texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Hilbert Transform on Graphs: Let There Be Phase
texts.text: Hilbert Transform on Graphs: Let There Be Phase

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Chun Hei Michael Chan, Student Member, IEEE, Alexandre Cionca, Student Member, IEEE,
texts.text: Chun Hei Michael Chan, Student Member, IEEE, Alexandre Cionca, Student Member, IEEE,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Dimitri Van De Ville, Fellow, IEEE
texts.text: Dimitri Van De Ville, Fellow, IEEE

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract -In the past years, many signal processing operations have been successfully adapted to the graph setting. One elegant and effective approach is to exploit the eigendecomposition of a graph shift operator (GSO), such as the adjacency or Laplacian operator, to define a graph Fourier transform for projecting graph signals on the corresponding basis. However, the extension of this scheme to directed graphs is challenging since the associated GSO is non-symmetric and, in general, not diagonalizable. Here, we build upon a recent framework that adds a minimal number of edges to allow diagonalization of the GSO and thus provide a proper graph Fourier transform. Furthermore, we show that such minimal addition of edges creates a cycle cover and that it is essential for the phase analysis of a signal throughout the graph. Concurrently, we propose a generalization of the Hilbert transform interpreted over the newfound cycle cover, which re-establishes intuitions from traditional Hilbert transform, equivalent to the generalized Hilbert transform on a single cycle. This generalization leads to a number of simple and elegant recipes to effectively exploit the phase information of graph signals provided by the graph Fourier transform. The feasibility of the approach is demonstrated on several examples.
texts.text: Abstract -In the past years, many signal processing operations have been successfully adapted to the graph setting. One elegant and effective approach is to exploit the eigendecomposition of a graph shift operator (GSO), such as the adjacency or Laplacian operator, to define a graph Fourier transform for projecting graph signals on the corresponding basis. However, the extension of this scheme to directed graphs is challenging since the associated GSO is non-symmetric and, in general, not diagonalizable. Here, we build upon a recent framework that adds a minimal number of edges to allow diagonalization of the GSO and thus provide a proper graph Fourier transform. Furthermore, we show that such minimal addition of edges creates a cycle cover and that it is essential for the phase analysis of a signal throughout the graph. Concurrently, we propose a generalization of the Hilbert transform interpreted over the newfound cycle cover, which re-establishes intuitions from traditional Hilbert transform, equivalent to the generalized Hilbert transform on a single cycle. This generalization leads to a number of simple and elegant recipes to effectively exploit the phase information of graph signals provided by the graph Fourier transform. The feasibility of the approach is demonstrated on several examples.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Index Terms -Graph signal processing, Harmonic analysis, Hilbert transform, Directed graph.
texts.text: Index Terms -Graph signal processing, Harmonic analysis, Hilbert transform, Directed graph.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: I. INTRODUCTION
texts.text: I. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: G RAPH signal processing (GSP) has emerged as an attractive new methodology for analyzing structured data [1]-[3]. In most cases, an undirected graph is considered, which implies a symmetric adjacency matrix. The definition of the graph Fourier transform (GFT) by the eigendecomposition of a graph shift operator (GSO) [4], commonly the adjacency or Laplacian matrix, leads then to real-valued eigenvalues and eigenvectors, and constitutes a unitary transform suitable for the analysis and synthesis of graph signals. Different operations, such as filtering, can then be defined and implemented in the spectral graph domain [5]-[7].
texts.text: G RAPH signal processing (GSP) has emerged as an attractive new methodology for analyzing structured data [1]-[3]. In most cases, an undirected graph is considered, which implies a symmetric adjacency matrix. The definition of the graph Fourier transform (GFT) by the eigendecomposition of a graph shift operator (GSO) [4], commonly the adjacency or Laplacian matrix, leads then to real-valued eigenvalues and eigenvectors, and constitutes a unitary transform suitable for the analysis and synthesis of graph signals. Different operations, such as filtering, can then be defined and implemented in the spectral graph domain [5]-[7].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For directed graphs, however, the eigendecomposition of the GSO leads to complex-valued eigenvalues and eigenvectors. Moreover the GSO itself becomes non-symmetric and is in general non-diagonalizable. While the Jordan normal form (JNF) has been suggested for providing the spectral decomposition of the GSO [8], this comes with several complications. First, the JNF is numerically unstable [9], especially for larger graphs. Second, non-trivial Jordan blocks correspond
texts.text: For directed graphs, however, the eigendecomposition of the GSO leads to complex-valued eigenvalues and eigenvectors. Moreover the GSO itself becomes non-symmetric and is in general non-diagonalizable. While the Jordan normal form (JNF) has been suggested for providing the spectral decomposition of the GSO [8], this comes with several complications. First, the JNF is numerically unstable [9], especially for larger graphs. Second, non-trivial Jordan blocks correspond

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Following these two main problems in the numerical application of the JNF in GSP, alternatives have been developed to ensure orthogonality through optimized bases [11], [12]. Both these works introduce directed total variation measures and yield real bases. Moreover, [12] finds bases with uniformly sampled frequency. However, both methods only work on real signals and bypass the GSO, meaning that only spectral operations would be allowed. Another approach proposed is to once again bypass the standard GSO and instead use the diagonalizable Hermitian Laplacian [13], which leads to a basis that satisfies Parseval property. These alternatives would fulfill properties such as orthogonality, evenly spread frequencies, and most importantly, diagonizability, but turn out to yield real eigenvalues, preventing the immediate definition of phase defined from complex values.
texts.text: Following these two main problems in the numerical application of the JNF in GSP, alternatives have been developed to ensure orthogonality through optimized bases [11], [12]. Both these works introduce directed total variation measures and yield real bases. Moreover, [12] finds bases with uniformly sampled frequency. However, both methods only work on real signals and bypass the GSO, meaning that only spectral operations would be allowed. Another approach proposed is to once again bypass the standard GSO and instead use the diagonalizable Hermitian Laplacian [13], which leads to a basis that satisfies Parseval property. These alternatives would fulfill properties such as orthogonality, evenly spread frequencies, and most importantly, diagonizability, but turn out to yield real eigenvalues, preventing the immediate definition of phase defined from complex values.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recent work allows to obtain both properties of diagonalizability and complex-valued eigendecomposition [14]. Specifically, by leveraging perturbation theory [15], [16] on the Jordan blocks, they propose to dismantle non-trivial Jordan blocks that are rank-2 or above, by adding a minimal number of edges to the directed graph. This method enables conventional diagonalization of the modified graph adjacency and thus permits the definition of the GFT as the projection on the dual basis derived from the GSO eigendecomposition. This method also yields a minimally different (spectrally) approximation of the original adjacency matrix [14]. The new adjacency remains a real-valued matrix that has an eigendecomposition with eigenvalues that are either real-valued or pairs of complex conjugates [17]. A parallel can thus be drawn between traditional discrete Fourier transform's (DFT) pairs of conjugate frequencies and the pairs of conjugate eigenvalues found in the presented eigendecomposition of the directed graph. A direct application of this property is the Hilbert transform.
texts.text: Recent work allows to obtain both properties of diagonalizability and complex-valued eigendecomposition [14]. Specifically, by leveraging perturbation theory [15], [16] on the Jordan blocks, they propose to dismantle non-trivial Jordan blocks that are rank-2 or above, by adding a minimal number of edges to the directed graph. This method enables conventional diagonalization of the modified graph adjacency and thus permits the definition of the GFT as the projection on the dual basis derived from the GSO eigendecomposition. This method also yields a minimally different (spectrally) approximation of the original adjacency matrix [14]. The new adjacency remains a real-valued matrix that has an eigendecomposition with eigenvalues that are either real-valued or pairs of complex conjugates [17]. A parallel can thus be drawn between traditional discrete Fourier transform's (DFT) pairs of conjugate frequencies and the pairs of conjugate eigenvalues found in the presented eigendecomposition of the directed graph. A direct application of this property is the Hilbert transform.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Prior work from [18] defines the graph Hilbert transform (GHT) through JNF, and while they show its equivalence to the traditional Hilbert transform, the authors revert to examples where diagonalizability and invertibility are satisfied, thus not giving a method to apply GHT on general graphs. On a nondiagonalizable graph, as we will see, the GHT through JNF does not provide phase information on all parts of the graph signal.
texts.text: Prior work from [18] defines the graph Hilbert transform (GHT) through JNF, and while they show its equivalence to the traditional Hilbert transform, the authors revert to examples where diagonalizability and invertibility are satisfied, thus not giving a method to apply GHT on general graphs. On a nondiagonalizable graph, as we will see, the GHT through JNF does not provide phase information on all parts of the graph signal.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Instead, here we propose using the mentioned minimal graph perturbation to render the GSO diagonalizable and invertible. While [14] only states that this minimal perturbation add cyclicity in the graph, we prove that it creates a cycle cover. Furthermore, we show through a simple example that such a cycle cover is essential for phase analysis throughout
texts.text: Instead, here we propose using the mentioned minimal graph perturbation to render the GSO diagonalizable and invertible. While [14] only states that this minimal perturbation add cyclicity in the graph, we prove that it creates a cycle cover. Furthermore, we show through a simple example that such a cycle cover is essential for phase analysis throughout

Leveraging this newfound cycle cover, we introduce a more intuitive and familiar definition of the GHT, providing interpretation for instantaneous phase and amplitude on cycles as in traditional HT. Finally we highlight its properties and specifically the extension to the graph analytical signal by insightful examples.
texts.text: the graph. Leveraging this newfound cycle cover, we introduce a more intuitive and familiar definition of the GHT, providing interpretation for instantaneous phase and amplitude on cycles as in traditional HT. Finally we highlight its properties and specifically the extension to the graph analytical signal by insightful examples.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: II. BACKGROUND
texts.text: II. BACKGROUND

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In what follows, we denote a matrix X as bold uppercase and a vector x as bold lowercase. An element k of a vector will be indicated as x [ k ] and a series of vectors x m will be indexed using subscript. The conjugate will be written as · ⋆ .
texts.text: In what follows, we denote a matrix X as bold uppercase and a vector x as bold lowercase. An element k of a vector will be indicated as x [ k ] and a series of vectors x m will be indexed using subscript. The conjugate will be written as · ⋆ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us consider a directed graph G = ( V, E ) , with V being the set of nodes and E the set of edges. The directed graph G has N nodes and is also characterized by the (nonsymmetric) adjacency matrix A . In [14], the perturbed GSO is obtained by adding iteratively rank-1 matrices (i.e., single edges) to A . On each iteration, the largest Jordan block is identified by considering the group of the largest number of collinear Jordan eigenvectors. Using [14, Th. 4, Lem. 12] a candidate edge to dismantle a Jordan block is an edge ( n, m ) such that | t [ n ] | | q [ m ] | = 0 , with t and q one of the left and right eigenvectors associated to the largest Jordan block, respectively. The same criterion is used on eigenvectors associated to the eigenvalue 0. At the end, the process yields a graph G ′ = ( V, E ′ ) with associated adjacency A ′ that is guaranteed to be diagonalizable and invertible.
texts.text: Let us consider a directed graph G = ( V, E ) , with V being the set of nodes and E the set of edges. The directed graph G has N nodes and is also characterized by the (nonsymmetric) adjacency matrix A . In [14], the perturbed GSO is obtained by adding iteratively rank-1 matrices (i.e., single edges) to A . On each iteration, the largest Jordan block is identified by considering the group of the largest number of collinear Jordan eigenvectors. Using [14, Th. 4, Lem. 12] a candidate edge to dismantle a Jordan block is an edge ( n, m ) such that | t [ n ] | | q [ m ] | = 0 , with t and q one of the left and right eigenvectors associated to the largest Jordan block, respectively. The same criterion is used on eigenvectors associated to the eigenvalue 0. At the end, the process yields a graph G ′ = ( V, E ′ ) with associated adjacency A ′ that is guaranteed to be diagonalizable and invertible.

texts.orig: where the eigenvalues Λ [ k, k ] = λ k are either real-valued with corresponding real-valued eigenvectors u k , k = 1 , . . . , N , that are the columns of U ; or complex-valued, and then come in pairs of complex conjugate eigenvalues and eigenvectors [17]. For instance, for a pair of complex-valued eigenvalues with indexes ( k 1 , k 2 ) , we have λ k 1 = λ k 2 ⋆ with eigenvectors u k 1 = u k 2 ⋆ . Consequently, the GFT ˆ x = U -1 x of a realvalued graph signal x ∈ R N will also have complex conjugate coefficients for these pairs; i.e., ˆ x [ k 1 ] = ˆ x [ k 2 ] ⋆ .
texts.text: where the eigenvalues Λ [ k, k ] = λ k are either real-valued with corresponding real-valued eigenvectors u k , k = 1 , . . . , N , that are the columns of U ; or complex-valued, and then come in pairs of complex conjugate eigenvalues and eigenvectors [17]. For instance, for a pair of complex-valued eigenvalues with indexes ( k 1 , k 2 ) , we have λ k 1 = λ k 2 ⋆ with eigenvectors u k 1 = u k 2 ⋆ . Consequently, the GFT ˆ x = U -1 x of a realvalued graph signal x ∈ R N will also have complex conjugate coefficients for these pairs; i.e., ˆ x [ k 1 ] = ˆ x [ k 2 ] ⋆ .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: B. Graph Hilbert Transform and Analytical Graph Signal
texts.text: B. Graph Hilbert Transform and Analytical Graph Signal

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We introduce the GHT by defining the following filter in the spectral domain by the diagonal matrix ˆ H :
texts.text: We introduce the GHT by defining the following filter in the spectral domain by the diagonal matrix ˆ H :

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ˆ H [ k, k ] =    -j, imag ( λ k ) > 0 , + j, imag ( λ k ) < 0 , 0 , imag ( λ k ) = 0 . (1)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thus GFT coefficients corresponding to complex-valued adjacency eigenvalues are multiplied with -j and + j according to the sign of the imaginary part of the eigenvalues. A positive/negative imaginary part of the eigenvalue can be interpreted as a positive/negative frequency. As aforementioned, GFT coefficients of a real-valued graph signal are complex conjugate for the pairs of complex eigenvalues. Pairs of conjugate coefficients, when multiplied by -j and + j , preserve conjugate relationship leading to a real synthesized signal. Therefore, the Hilbert transform H ( x ) = U ˆ HU -1 x of such a graph signal is also real-valued. Subsequently, we can define the equivalent of the analytical signal in traditional Hilbert transform [19] to graph, namely the analytical graph signal:
texts.text: Thus GFT coefficients corresponding to complex-valued adjacency eigenvalues are multiplied with -j and + j according to the sign of the imaginary part of the eigenvalues. A positive/negative imaginary part of the eigenvalue can be interpreted as a positive/negative frequency. As aforementioned, GFT coefficients of a real-valued graph signal are complex conjugate for the pairs of complex eigenvalues. Pairs of conjugate coefficients, when multiplied by -j and + j , preserve conjugate relationship leading to a real synthesized signal. Therefore, the Hilbert transform H ( x ) = U ˆ HU -1 x of such a graph signal is also real-valued. Subsequently, we can define the equivalent of the analytical signal in traditional Hilbert transform [19] to graph, namely the analytical graph signal:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ˜ x = x + j H ( x ) = U ( I + j ˆ H )ˆ x , (2)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: which can be represented in terms of instantaneous amplitudes and phases:
texts.text: which can be represented in terms of instantaneous amplitudes and phases:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: A ( x )[ k ] = ∥ ˜ x [ k ] ∥ , φ ( x )[ k ] = arctan ( Im (˜ x [ k ]) , Re (˜ x [ k ])) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The analytical signal is employed in amplitude and frequency modulation (AM/FM) [20]. In a similar fashion, the analytical graph signal sets the ground for graph-wide amplitude and frequency modulation.
texts.text: The analytical signal is employed in amplitude and frequency modulation (AM/FM) [20]. In a similar fashion, the analytical graph signal sets the ground for graph-wide amplitude and frequency modulation.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: C. Interpretation of Graph Hilbert Transform
texts.text: C. Interpretation of Graph Hilbert Transform

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In traditional signal processing, the Hilbert transform of a signal provides the magnitude of its envelope and phase of its oscillatory pattern. This interpretation directly applies to a directed cycle graph, which is a model for a 1-D discrete signal with periodic boundary conditions. For a more general graph, we need to acknowledge its structure in terms of subcycles. The described method of minimal perturbation allows us to infer the existence of these subcycles, where each one satisfies periodic boundary conditions. With the following Proposition and Corollary, we put forward a generalized interpretation of the GHT instantaneous amplitude and phase.
texts.text: In traditional signal processing, the Hilbert transform of a signal provides the magnitude of its envelope and phase of its oscillatory pattern. This interpretation directly applies to a directed cycle graph, which is a model for a 1-D discrete signal with periodic boundary conditions. For a more general graph, we need to acknowledge its structure in terms of subcycles. The described method of minimal perturbation allows us to infer the existence of these subcycles, where each one satisfies periodic boundary conditions. With the following Proposition and Corollary, we put forward a generalized interpretation of the GHT instantaneous amplitude and phase.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us recall that a graph G is r -acyclic if any collection of vertex-disjoint cycles of G covers at most N -r nodes. A cycle cover is a collection of cycles of G that includes all nodes of G . We also bring up the following definition and theorem [21].
texts.text: Let us recall that a graph G is r -acyclic if any collection of vertex-disjoint cycles of G covers at most N -r nodes. A cycle cover is a collection of cycles of G that includes all nodes of G . We also bring up the following definition and theorem [21].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 1. [21] The set S G of matrices associated to subgraphs of G : S G = { A sub s.t G sub = ( V, E sub ) and E sub ⊆ E } .
texts.text: Definition 1. [21] The set S G of matrices associated to subgraphs of G : S G = { A sub s.t G sub = ( V, E sub ) and E sub ⊆ E } .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 1. [21, Th. 4.4] A graph G is r -acyclic if and only if every matrix A sub ∈ S G has at least r zero eigenvalues.
texts.text: Theorem 1. [21, Th. 4.4] A graph G is r -acyclic if and only if every matrix A sub ∈ S G has at least r zero eigenvalues.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proposition 1. G ′ admits a cycle cover; i.e., it can be decomposed into M subcycles C 1 , . . . , C M such that ⋃ M m =1 C m = V .
texts.text: Proposition 1. G ′ admits a cycle cover; i.e., it can be decomposed into M subcycles C 1 , . . . , C M such that ⋃ M m =1 C m = V .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Let us first show that the graph G ′ is 0 -acyclic. By contradiction, let us assume that our graph G ′ is r -acyclic with r = 0 . By using Theorem. 1 in the direct sense, we have that every matrix A sub ∈ S G ′ has at least one zero eigenvalue. However, the associated adjacency matrix A ′ ∈ S G ′ has no zero eigenvalues due to invertibility. This leads to a contradiction and, therefore, G ′ can only be 0 -acyclic, consequently, G ′ admits a cycle cover.
texts.text: Proof. Let us first show that the graph G ′ is 0 -acyclic. By contradiction, let us assume that our graph G ′ is r -acyclic with r = 0 . By using Theorem. 1 in the direct sense, we have that every matrix A sub ∈ S G ′ has at least one zero eigenvalue. However, the associated adjacency matrix A ′ ∈ S G ′ has no zero eigenvalues due to invertibility. This leads to a contradiction and, therefore, G ′ can only be 0 -acyclic, consequently, G ′ admits a cycle cover.

texts.orig: These properties explain how the signals from different cycles combine for overlapping nodes; i.e., nodes of overlapping subcycles. For a node k that exclusively belongs to one subcycle C m , we have x [ k ] = x m [ k ] and thus the equations revert to the conventional interpretation of instantaneous amplitude and phase in that cycle. For nodes of overlapping subcycles, the strengths of the contributing signals x m will determine their contribution.
texts.text: These properties explain how the signals from different cycles combine for overlapping nodes; i.e., nodes of overlapping subcycles. For a node k that exclusively belongs to one subcycle C m , we have x [ k ] = x m [ k ] and thus the equations revert to the conventional interpretation of instantaneous amplitude and phase in that cycle. For nodes of overlapping subcycles, the strengths of the contributing signals x m will determine their contribution.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 2) Experimental Data: From the Manhattan graph [22], we take the midtown subgraph composed of many one-way avenues and streets (in both directions), see Fig. 3a. We also created a graph from a regular 2D grid of similar size where the nodes are connected from top-to-bottom and leftto-right with periodic boundary conditions, see Fig. 3b. As a graph signal, we created a planar wave pattern along the direction of the avenues (i.e., approximately north-south). The wave is that of a sinusoid and the frequency of the wave is chosen such that one period covers the midtown area, this yields a sinusoidal plane wave. The graph signal is illustrated in Fig. 3c, 3e on the regular 2D grid and midtown Manhattan graph, respectively. The chosen graphs and signals reflect domains and monogenic signals where traditional HT is commonly applied: Fig. 1a evokes temporal applications with overlapping nodes, Fig. 3b recalls image-based uses via a lattice structure, and the Manhattan graph Fig. 3a combines grid-like topology with real graph directionality.
texts.text: 2) Experimental Data: From the Manhattan graph [22], we take the midtown subgraph composed of many one-way avenues and streets (in both directions), see Fig. 3a. We also created a graph from a regular 2D grid of similar size where the nodes are connected from top-to-bottom and leftto-right with periodic boundary conditions, see Fig. 3b. As a graph signal, we created a planar wave pattern along the direction of the avenues (i.e., approximately north-south). The wave is that of a sinusoid and the frequency of the wave is chosen such that one period covers the midtown area, this yields a sinusoidal plane wave. The graph signal is illustrated in Fig. 3c, 3e on the regular 2D grid and midtown Manhattan graph, respectively. The chosen graphs and signals reflect domains and monogenic signals where traditional HT is commonly applied: Fig. 1a evokes temporal applications with overlapping nodes, Fig. 3b recalls image-based uses via a lattice structure, and the Manhattan graph Fig. 3a combines grid-like topology with real graph directionality.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: B. Results
texts.text: B. Results

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 1) Synthetic Data: We instantiate the graph of Fig. 1a for N C = 20 fans of length N F = 20 . The adjacency matrix is neither diagonizable nor invertible, with the Jordan block decomposition providing N C blocks with eigenvalues zero. Removing the non-trivial Jordan blocks leads to the directed rosace graph as shown schematically in Fig. 1b, turning fans into subcycles linked to the central part.
texts.text: 1) Synthetic Data: We instantiate the graph of Fig. 1a for N C = 20 fans of length N F = 20 . The adjacency matrix is neither diagonizable nor invertible, with the Jordan block decomposition providing N C blocks with eigenvalues zero. Removing the non-trivial Jordan blocks leads to the directed rosace graph as shown schematically in Fig. 1b, turning fans into subcycles linked to the central part.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where k +1 indicates the next node on the fan and l is the integer that turns ω in the interval ] -π, π ] following Itoh's unwrapping method [23]. As shown in Figs. 2a and 2b, the average amplitude and frequency per fan are accurate estimates of the ground truth expressed in the synthetic graph signal. In addition, the instantaneous amplitude on the central cycle recovers the envelope of the oscillating and increasing signal, and the phase varies constantly since the frequency is fixed on the central cycle (Fig. 2c).
texts.text: where k +1 indicates the next node on the fan and l is the integer that turns ω in the interval ] -π, π ] following Itoh's unwrapping method [23]. As shown in Figs. 2a and 2b, the average amplitude and frequency per fan are accurate estimates of the ground truth expressed in the synthetic graph signal. In addition, the instantaneous amplitude on the central cycle recovers the envelope of the oscillating and increasing signal, and the phase varies constantly since the frequency is fixed on the central cycle (Fig. 2c).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This example perfectly illustrates Proposition 1. First, the GHT is fully in line with the classical Hilbert transform on the fans that form non-overlapping cycles, and thus amplitude, phase, and frequency are perfectly recovered. Second, the GHT has the capability to provide similar properties for an overlapping cycle (i.e., the central cycle of this graph). It is clear that the graph signal expressed on the central cycle is 'compatible' with the one on the fans. Nevertheless, instantaneous amplitude and phase are in accordance with the meaning of the conventional Hilbert transform.
texts.text: This example perfectly illustrates Proposition 1. First, the GHT is fully in line with the classical Hilbert transform on the fans that form non-overlapping cycles, and thus amplitude, phase, and frequency are perfectly recovered. Second, the GHT has the capability to provide similar properties for an overlapping cycle (i.e., the central cycle of this graph). It is clear that the graph signal expressed on the central cycle is 'compatible' with the one on the fans. Nevertheless, instantaneous amplitude and phase are in accordance with the meaning of the conventional Hilbert transform.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 2) Experimental Data: The adjustment of the adjacency matrix of the Manhattan midtown graph leads to 252 extra edges (compared to the 1970 original ones), indicated in green in Fig. 3a. The graph based on the regular 2D grid does not require any adjustment (Fig. 3b). First, we look at the effect of the GHT on the planar wave graph signal expressed on the regular grid (Fig. 3d). We observe a phase shift of π/ 2 along the propagation direction, turning the sine into a cosine. Second, the GHT of the same signal expressed on the Manhattan midtown graph produces a complicated scattering of wave (Fig. 3f) due to the intricate pattern of directed edges (by one-way streets and avenues crossing, see Fig. 3a). We can recognize different patterns in the east and west of the midtown. The implementation is publicly available 1 .
texts.text: 2) Experimental Data: The adjustment of the adjacency matrix of the Manhattan midtown graph leads to 252 extra edges (compared to the 1970 original ones), indicated in green in Fig. 3a. The graph based on the regular 2D grid does not require any adjustment (Fig. 3b). First, we look at the effect of the GHT on the planar wave graph signal expressed on the regular grid (Fig. 3d). We observe a phase shift of π/ 2 along the propagation direction, turning the sine into a cosine. Second, the GHT of the same signal expressed on the Manhattan midtown graph produces a complicated scattering of wave (Fig. 3f) due to the intricate pattern of directed edges (by one-way streets and avenues crossing, see Fig. 3a). We can recognize different patterns in the east and west of the midtown. The implementation is publicly available 1 .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: V. DISCUSSION & OUTLOOK
texts.text: V. DISCUSSION & OUTLOOK

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While the Hilbert transform has been extended previously to the directed graph setting [18] through the JNF, we highlight here that it is key to first have the eigendecomposition of the GSO to be well-defined; i.e., diagonalizable and invertible, and, consequently, to have a cycle cover in order to enable phase analysis across the graph. In addition, we provide an interpretation of the GHT by constructing the graph signal from contributions on subcycles. This leads to a useful intuition on how the GHT combines information from different cycles. Our first example illustrates how the GHT matches the conventional Hilbert transform on non-overlapping cycles, and can remain interpretable on overlapping ones. On a graph that corresponds to a regular 2-D grid, the GHT behaves as expected from monogenic signal analysis [24], [25]; that is, the phase shift of the Hilbert transform occurs along the propagation direction of the planar wave. However, when applying the GHT on the more general Manhattan midtown graph where the distribution of directions is non-uniform, the complexity of the graph structure is reflected in an intricate pattern that is phase-shifted differently in different parts.
texts.text: While the Hilbert transform has been extended previously to the directed graph setting [18] through the JNF, we highlight here that it is key to first have the eigendecomposition of the GSO to be well-defined; i.e., diagonalizable and invertible, and, consequently, to have a cycle cover in order to enable phase analysis across the graph. In addition, we provide an interpretation of the GHT by constructing the graph signal from contributions on subcycles. This leads to a useful intuition on how the GHT combines information from different cycles. Our first example illustrates how the GHT matches the conventional Hilbert transform on non-overlapping cycles, and can remain interpretable on overlapping ones. On a graph that corresponds to a regular 2-D grid, the GHT behaves as expected from monogenic signal analysis [24], [25]; that is, the phase shift of the Hilbert transform occurs along the propagation direction of the planar wave. However, when applying the GHT on the more general Manhattan midtown graph where the distribution of directions is non-uniform, the complexity of the graph structure is reflected in an intricate pattern that is phase-shifted differently in different parts.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In terms of signal representation for GHT, if priors on subcycles' signal are unavailable, we propose that representation is best achieved through decomposition into a weighted sum of GSO eigenvectors, with cycle decompositions ensuring near-linear instantaneous phase for each eigenvectors, akin to all Fourier bases being of linear phase [26]. While such decomposition is natural, further study is needed and we believe this merits separate work along a focused investigation on real-world applications.
texts.text: In terms of signal representation for GHT, if priors on subcycles' signal are unavailable, we propose that representation is best achieved through decomposition into a weighted sum of GSO eigenvectors, with cycle decompositions ensuring near-linear instantaneous phase for each eigenvectors, akin to all Fourier bases being of linear phase [26]. While such decomposition is natural, further study is needed and we believe this merits separate work along a focused investigation on real-world applications.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, we used the adjacency matrix as the GSO. However, the directed Laplacian [27] is also a valid choice and in particular other novel decomposition could allow for phase definitions, such as the use of the polar decomposition, by looking into the introduced unitary matrix Q [28] and the Schur decomposition which exhibits real and imaginary valued blocks in its upper quasi-triangular matrix [29].
texts.text: In this work, we used the adjacency matrix as the GSO. However, the directed Laplacian [27] is also a valid choice and in particular other novel decomposition could allow for phase definitions, such as the use of the polar decomposition, by looking into the introduced unitary matrix Q [28] and the Schur decomposition which exhibits real and imaginary valued blocks in its upper quasi-triangular matrix [29].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Future work includes extending the known applications of the HT [20], [30], [31] to directed graphs. In particular the GHT can analyze amplitude or frequency modulation in graph signals, such as synchrony of wave-like patterns encountered in neuronal recordings on irregular domains [32].
texts.text: Future work includes extending the known applications of the HT [20], [30], [31] to directed graphs. In particular the GHT can analyze amplitude or frequency modulation in graph signals, such as synchrony of wave-like patterns encountered in neuronal recordings on irregular domains [32].
